subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5! In this unit you will grow into a confident, careful calculator who can add and subtract decimal numbers, work with positive and negative numbers, and even solve puzzles where numbers are hidden behind symbols. Addition and subtraction are the two oldest tools in mathematics, and you already use them every single day. When Amina counts out shillings at the Hargeisa market to pay for mangoes, when Hassan checks how much fuel is left after a long drive to Kismaayo, or when Khadija works out how many degrees the temperature fell overnight, they are all adding and subtracting.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Read, write and understand decimal numbers and what each place is worth.
Estimate the answer to a calculation before you work it out exactly.
Add and subtract decimals by lining up the decimal points and carrying or borrowing.
Break a decimal calculation into parts using the partitioning method.
Understand positive and negative numbers and place them on a number line.
Add and subtract positive and negative numbers using a number line.
Solve problems with symbols and unknowns, such as finding the value of a shape.
Apply all of these skills to real-world problems: money, temperature, and more.
Check every answer by estimating and by asking whether it makes sense.

concepts.title: Understanding Decimal Numbers
concepts.explanation: A decimal number is a number that includes a decimal point – a small dot that separates the whole-number part from the part that is smaller than one. You see decimals everywhere. A price of $4.50 means 4 whole dollars and 50 cents. A height of 2.75 metres means 2 whole metres and 75 hundredths of a metre. A gymnastics score of 9.25 points means 9 whole points plus a quarter of a point more. The easiest way to understand decimals is to think about money, because you already know money well. In the amount $12.34,…
concepts.example: Just as with whole numbers, place value tells us what each digit is really worth. The places to the right of the decimal point have special names. The first place after the point is the tenths (one whole split into 10…

concepts.title: Estimating Before You Calculate
concepts.explanation: Before you work out any calculation exactly, you should always make an estimate. An estimate is a quick, sensible guess of the answer, usually made by rounding each number to the nearest whole number. Estimating first is a habit that good mathematicians keep for their whole lives, because it acts like a safety net: if your careful answer lands far from your estimate, you know at once that something went wrong and you should check your work. Suppose you must calculate 9.75 + 13.42. Before touching the exact digits,…
concepts.example: Estimating works for subtraction too. For 15.60 − 7.25, round to 16 − 7 = 9. The real answer, 8.35, is close to 9, so it passes the check. Notice that an estimate is not meant to be exactly right – it is meant to be…

concepts.title: Adding Decimal Numbers
concepts.explanation: Adding decimals is almost the same as adding whole numbers, with one golden rule: line up the decimal points. When the decimal points sit in a straight vertical line, every place value lines up too – ones under ones, tenths under tenths, hundredths under hundredths – and then you simply add each column, carrying when a column goes past nine, exactly as you already know how to do. Let us add 9.75 + 13.42 step by step, the careful way:
concepts.example: Estimate first: 10 + 13 = 23, so the answer should be near 23.

concepts.title: Subtracting Decimal Numbers
concepts.explanation: Subtracting decimals follows the very same golden rule: line up the decimal points, then subtract column by column from the right, borrowing when the top digit is too small, just as you do with whole numbers. Let us subtract 15.60 − 7.25: Estimate first: 16 − 7 = 9, so the answer should be near 9.
concepts.example: Line up the decimal points, the larger number on top.

concepts.title: Positive and Negative Numbers
concepts.explanation: So far every number has been zero or above. But numbers also go below zero. A negative number is a number less than zero, and we write it with a small minus sign in front, like −5. Negative numbers are not strange or unusual – they describe everyday things. The temperature −5°C means five degrees below freezing. A basement floor labelled −2 is two floors below the ground. A bank balance of −$20 means you owe twenty dollars because you spent more than you had. A positive number is a number greater than zero, such…
concepts.example: Think of an elevator in a tall building in Nairobi. The ground floor is 0. The floors above ground are the positive numbers: 1, 2, 3 and so on, going up. The floors below ground, in the car park, are the negative…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting with a Number Line
concepts.explanation: The number line is not just for picturing numbers – it is a machine for adding and subtracting them, including negatives. The rule is simple and worth memorising: To add a positive number, move right on the number line.
concepts.example: To add a negative number, move left on the number line.

methods.title: How to add or subtract two decimals
steps: Estimate first by rounding each number to the nearest whole.
Write the numbers with the decimal points lined up vertically.
Add zeros on the right so both have the same number of places.
Work from the right, carrying or borrowing as needed.
Put the decimal point in the answer, in line with the others, then check against your estimate.

methods.title: How to add or subtract with negatives
steps: Draw or picture a number line with 0 in the middle.
Start at the first number.
For adding a positive, move right; for adding a negative or subtracting, move left.
Count the steps carefully and read off where you land.

methods.title: How to solve a two-symbol puzzle
methods.example: Sahra goes shopping with $50.00. She buys a book for $12.75, a pen for $3.50 and lunch for $8.25. How much did she spend, and how much change does she get?
steps: Write down both clues, such as square + triangle and square − triangle.
Add the two clues so one symbol cancels out, giving double the other symbol.
Find that symbol, then use a clue to find the second symbol.
Check your answers by putting them back into both clues.

methods.title: Order numbers with negatives
methods.example: Put these in order from smallest to largest: −3, 5, −1, 0, −5, 2.
steps: Solution: Picture the number line.
The most negative number is furthest left, so it is smallest.
Reading left to right: −5, −3, −1, 0, 2, 5.
Remember −5 is smaller than −3, even though 5 is bigger than 3, because it is further below zero.

methods.title: Add on a number line
methods.example: Use a number line to work out −4 + 7.
steps: Solution: Start at −4.
You are adding a positive 7, so move 7 spaces to the right: −4, −3, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, 3.
You land on 3.
Check: from −4, moving up 4 reaches 0, and 3 more reaches 3, using all 7 steps.

methods.title: A temperature problem
methods.example: The temperature was 3°C in the morning. During the day it rose by 8°C, then dropped by 12°C overnight. What was the temperature after rising, and what was the final temperature?
steps: Solution: After rising: 3 + 8 = 11°C.
Then dropping 12: start at 11 and move 12 left: 11 − 12 = −1°C.
The final temperature is one degree below freezing.
Check on the number line: from 11, going down 11 reaches 0, and one more step reaches −1.

workedExamples.title: Add two decimals
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate first: 13 + 9 = 22, so expect about 22. Line up the points and add from the right: hundredths 5 + 0 = 5; tenths 7 + 5 = 12 (write 2, carry 1); ones 2 + 8 + 1 = 11 (write 1, carry 1); tens 1 + 0 + 1 = 2. Answer: 21.25. Check: 21.25 is close to 22, so it makes sense.

workedExamples.title: Subtract two decimals
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate: 26 − 18 = 8, so expect about 8. Line up the points. Hundredths: 0 − 5 needs a borrow, so 10 − 5 = 5. Tenths: after borrowing, 5 − 2 = 3. Ones and tens: 25 becomes what remains, giving 7 in the ones. Answer: 7.35. Check: 7.35 is close to 8. Correct.

workedExamples.title: A money problem
workedExamples.prompt: Sahra goes shopping with $50.00. She buys a book for $12.75, a pen for $3.50 and lunch for $8.25. How much did she spend, and how much change does she get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the costs: 12.75 + 3.50 + 8.25 = $24.50 spent. Then subtract from her money: 50.00 − 24.50 = $25.50 change. Check by estimating: about 13 + 4 + 8 = 25 spent, and 50 − 25 = 25 change. Both match, so the answer makes sense.

workedExamples.title: Order numbers with negatives
workedExamples.prompt: Put these in order from smallest to largest: −3, 5, −1, 0, −5, 2.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Picture the number line. The most negative number is furthest left, so it is smallest. Reading left to right: −5, −3, −1, 0, 2, 5. Remember −5 is smaller than −3, even though 5 is bigger than 3, because it is further below zero.

workedExamples.title: Add on a number line
workedExamples.prompt: Use a number line to work out −4 + 7.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start at −4. You are adding a positive 7, so move 7 spaces to the right: −4, −3, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, 3. You land on 3. Check: from −4, moving up 4 reaches 0, and 3 more reaches 3, using all 7 steps. Correct.

workedExamples.title: A temperature problem
workedExamples.prompt: The temperature was 3°C in the morning. During the day it rose by 8°C, then dropped by 12°C overnight. What was the temperature after rising, and what was the final temperature?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: After rising: 3 + 8 = 11°C. Then dropping 12: start at 11 and move 12 left: 11 − 12 = −1°C. The final temperature is one degree below freezing. Check on the number line: from 11, going down 11 reaches 0, and one more step reaches −1. Correct.

workedExamples.title: A symbol puzzle
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the two clues together so the triangles cancel: (■ + ▲) + (■ − ▲) = 20 + 6, giving ■ + ■ = 26, so ■ = 13. Then from ■ + ▲ = 20, the triangle is 20 − 13 = 7. Check: 13 + 7 = 20 and 13 − 7 = 6. Both clues work.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step bank problem
workedExamples.prompt: A bank account starts at $75.50. There is a deposit of $25.00, then a withdrawal of $18.75, then a withdrawal of $12.50. Find the balance after each step.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: After the deposit: 75.50 + 25.00 = $100.50. After the first withdrawal: 100.50 − 18.75 = $81.75. After the second withdrawal: 81.75 − 12.50 = $69.25. Each step is a single addition or subtraction, and the running balance tells the whole story.

workedExamples.title: Find the missing number
workedExamples.prompt: Find the missing number: a) 7.5 + ___ = 12.8 b) ___ − 6.25 = 8.50

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: An elevator starts at ground floor (0). It goes up 8 floors, then down 12 floors. Which floor is it on now?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: If ★ + ★ + ★ = 21, what is ★? Then, if ★ + ● = 12, what is ●?

activities.title: Decimal Detective Shopping Spree
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and a calculator for checking only.
steps: Design your own store and write a price list of at least 10 items using decimal prices, such as a ball $3.25 or a puzzle $12.75.
You have $25.00 for a party. Choose three items, add up the cost, and work out your change.
Sale day: every item is $2.50 off. Pick five items and calculate their new prices, then how much you saved in total.
Store manager challenge: your sales today were $23.75, $8.50, $45.25, $12.00 and $67.80. Find the total, then how much more you need to reach a $150.00 goal.
Always estimate each total first, then add carefully with the decimal points lined up.

activities.title: Temperature Adventure Around the World
steps: You are a weather reporter for these cities: Nairobi 28°C, Hargeisa 24°C, Moscow −15°C, Antarctica −40°C, London 2°C.
Draw a number line from −40 to 30 and mark each city on it.
Work out how much warmer Nairobi is than Moscow, and the gap between the hottest and coldest places.
If London's temperature drops by 8 degrees, what will it be? Show it on your number line.
Subtracting a negative feels tricky, so use your number line and count the steps between the two temperatures.

activities.title: Number Line Adventure Game
activities.materials: a long strip of paper, a ruler, and a small game piece such as a button.
steps: Make a large number line from −20 to +20 and mark every number clearly.
Start at 0 and follow moves: right 5, left 8, right 3, left 12. Where do you finish?
Treasure hunt: hide 'treasures' at different numbers and write six moves that lead a friend to one.
Elevator ride: treat the line as floors. Start at 0, go up 7, down 10, up 4, down 6. Which floor are you on?

activities.title: Bank Account Manager
activities.materials: paper, pencil, coloured pens and a calculator for checking.
steps: Make a bank statement with columns for Date, Description, Amount and New Balance. Start with a balance of $100.00.
Colour money coming in green and money going out red, and work out the new balance after each line.
What is your final balance? Did the balance ever come close to going negative? What would a negative balance mean?

activities.title: Mystery Symbol Solver
activities.materials: paper and coloured pencils for the different symbols.
steps: Solve: ★ + ★ = 16. Draw your thinking and check your answer.
Solve the pair: ♥ + ♦ = 15 and ♥ − ♦ = 3. What are ♥ and ♦?
Real-world puzzle: an apple plus a banana costs $4.50, and an apple minus a banana is $1.50. How much is each fruit?
Design three symbol puzzles of your own for a friend, and write an answer key with explanations.

activities.title: Sports Statistics Analyst
activities.materials: paper, pencil and coloured pencils for charts.
steps: A team scored these points in seven games: 78.5, 82.0, 75.5, 88.0, 79.5, 85.5, 91.0. Find the total and the gap between their highest and lowest games.
A swimmer's 100 m times were 58.75, 57.50, 58.25, 56.75, 57.00 seconds. Work out the change from week to week and find their best time.
For each game, work out how many points above or below 80 the team scored, using positive and negative numbers.
Make a colourful chart of the results and write a short report on what the numbers show.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which was easier to work with, decimals or negative numbers, and why? When might you use decimal addition and subtraction in real life? How does a number line help you understand negative numbers? Which activity taught you the most? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Decimal Adding and Subtracting Rule
rules.text: Line up the decimal points, not the last digits. Add zeros on the right so both numbers are the same length. Estimate first, then add or subtract each column from the right.

rules.title: Number Line Rule
rules.text: Numbers get bigger to the right and smaller to the left. Add a positive: move right. Add a negative or subtract: move left. You can subtract a bigger number from a smaller one; the answer is negative.

rules.title: Place Value Rule
rules.text: First place after the point = tenths. Second place = hundredths. 0.5 and 0.50 are equal. 0.5 is bigger than 0.05.

terms: A number with a point showing parts of a whole
The dot between the wholes and the parts
The first and second places after the point
A sensible guess before calculating exactly
A line of numbers in order, used to calculate
A missing value shown by a symbol or shape
commonMistakes: Line up the decimal points instead
0.5 is five tenths, bigger than five hundredths
Moving the wrong way for negatives
Add positive → right; add negative → left
Cross zero; the answer is negative

realProblems.prompt: Bank account: start $125.50, deposit $45.75, withdraw $32.25, withdraw $18.50. What is the final balance?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A mountain climber starts at base camp (0 m). She climbs up 1,250 m, then down 300 m, then up 450 m. What is her final elevation?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Weather in one week: Mon 15°C, Tue 18°C, Wed 12°C, Thu 8°C, Fri −2°C. What is the temperature change from Monday to Friday?
realProblems.answer: From Monday (15°C) to Friday (−2°C): −2 − 15 = −17°C change (a drop of 17°C).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: From Monday (15°C) to Friday (−2°C): −2 − 15 = −17°C change (a drop of 17°C).

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: If ★ + ★ + ★ = 21, what is ★? Then, if ★ + ● = 12, what is ●?
If ■ + ▲ = 20 and ■ − ▲ = 6, find ■ and ▲. Show your method.
Magic idea: start at 5°C. It drops 8°C, rises 3°C, then drops 2°C. What is the final temperature?
Two apples and one banana cost $3.50. One apple costs $1.25. How much does the banana cost?
A store takes $5.50 off any item over $20.00. What do you pay for an item priced at $32.75?
Explain the key idea in Adding and Subtracting with a Number Line.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The number line is not just for picturing numbers – it is a machine for adding and subtracting them, including negatives. The rule is simple and worth memorising: To add a positive number, move right on the number line.

questions.question: What does “Decimal number” mean?
options: A number with a point showing parts of a whole
The dot between the wholes and the parts
The first and second places after the point
A sensible guess before calculating exactly
questions.answer: A number with a point showing parts of a whole
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.

selfAssessment: I can read, write and understand decimal numbers and what each place is worth.
I can estimate the answer to a calculation before you work it out exactly.
I can add and subtract decimals by lining up the decimal points and carrying or borrowing.
I can break a decimal calculation into parts using the partitioning method.
I can understand positive and negative numbers and place them on a number line.
I can add and subtract positive and negative numbers using a number line.
I can solve problems with symbols and unknowns, such as finding the value of a shape.
I can apply all of these skills to real-world problems: money, temperature, and more.
